name: Kris Kobach
occupation: Attorney General of Kansas; former law professor, immigration attorney
hometown: Farm near Lecompton, Kansas (raised in Topeka)
education: Harvard University (B.A. in Government, summa cum laude); University of Oxford (Ph.D. in Politics, Marshall Scholar); Yale Law School (J.D.)
family: Wife Heather; five daughters: Josephine, Lilly, Charlotte, Reagan, and Molly (all homeschooled)
margin2024: Won 2022 AG race 50.8%-49.1% (1.7-point margin, closest statewide race that cycle)
whoTheyAre:
Kris Kobach grew up in Topeka, graduated from Washburn Rural High School, and went on to earn degrees from three of the most prestigious universities in the world: a bachelor's from Harvard (where he graduated first in his department), a doctorate in politics from Oxford on a Marshall Scholarship, and a law degree from Yale. He clerked for a federal appeals court judge in Lawrence, served as a White House Fellow under Attorney General John Ashcroft after 9/11, and spent 15 years as a constitutional law professor at UMKC. He lives on a farm near Lecompton with his wife Heather and their five daughters, all of whom are homeschooled.

Before becoming attorney general, Kobach was already one of the most recognized names in Kansas politics. He served on the Overland Park City Council, ran for Congress in 2004, and then won two terms as Kansas Secretary of State from 2011 to 2019. During that time he became a national figure in the debates over voter fraud and immigration enforcement. He ran for governor in 2018, winning a razor-thin Republican primary over Jeff Colyer by about 345 votes, but lost the general election to Democrat Laura Kelly. He ran for U.S. Senate in 2020 and lost the primary to Roger Marshall. In 2022, he won the attorney general's race by just 1.7 points over Democrat Chris Mann -- the closest statewide race in Kansas that year.

Kobach has built his entire public identity around a handful of issues: election integrity, immigration enforcement, and conservative legal fights against the federal government. He is deeply polarizing. Supporters see him as one of the most principled conservative lawyers in America. Critics say he picks headline-grabbing fights, loses in court, and leaves taxpayers with the bill.
theirRecord:
As attorney general since January 2023, Kobach has used the office to file or join lawsuits on the biggest conservative legal fights in the country. He joined coalitions challenging Biden administration policies on immigration, student loan forgiveness, DACA health insurance, and Title IX transgender protections -- and won preliminary injunctions or outright victories in several of those cases. He filed a consumer protection lawsuit against Pfizer alleging the company misled the public about its COVID-19 vaccine, and won the battle to keep that case in Kansas state court. He led Kansas into a 13-state lawsuit against major investment firms over ESG policies, which resulted in a $29.5 million settlement from Vanguard. He also joined a 50-state coalition that secured a $149 million settlement from Mercedes-Benz for unfair trade practices.

On crime, Kobach created the Joint Fentanyl Impact Team, deployed the first fentanyl-detecting K-9 officers in Kansas, and secured $6.9 million in state funding and $8.5 million in addiction treatment grants distributed to 32 organizations. His office points to Kansas crime dropping to its lowest rate in 20 years during his tenure, though the attorney general's direct role in local crime rates is limited, and the decline mirrors a broader national trend.

Kobach's record also includes significant losses and controversies. He lost the transgender driver's license gender marker case on appeal, with the Kansas Court of Appeals finding he had not shown a likelihood of winning. A judge fined him a symbolic $1 for filing a motion in a court without jurisdiction, saying he "should have known better." He fired a law firm handling a state natural gas price-gouging case, allegedly because its founder donated to Democrats, and had to reverse course after legal experts warned of retaliation liability. His office directed raids on THC smoke shops in October 2025 that led to three businesses suing the state for alleged illegal search and seizure. His ongoing power struggle with Governor Kelly over SNAP data resulted in dueling lawsuits, both of which were ultimately dismissed. His earlier record as secretary of state remains a major part of his story: his proof-of-citizenship voter law was struck down in federal court after blocking more than 35,000 eligible Kansas voters while catching only 39 noncitizens. He was held in contempt of court, ordered to pay $26,215 in sanctions, and ordered to take remedial law classes on civil procedure -- an extraordinary rebuke for a lawyer with his credentials. The total cost to Kansas taxpayers from that litigation exceeded $4 million.
whatYouShouldKnow: Kobach was held in contempt of federal court in 2018 for violating a judge's order to register voters. He was fined $26,215 and ordered to complete six hours of remedial legal education classes on basic civil procedure -- despite holding degrees from Harvard, Oxford, and Yale. The judge also sanctioned him separately for attempting to introduce evidence at trial that he had concealed from opposing counsel.

His signature law as secretary of state -- requiring documentary proof of citizenship to register to vote -- was struck down as unconstitutional after a federal trial found it blocked more than 35,000 eligible Kansas citizens from registering while catching only 39 noncitizens over nearly 20 years. The judge wrote: "There is no iceberg; only an icicle, largely created by confusion and administrative error." The litigation cost Kansas taxpayers more than $4 million.

He served as vice chair of President Trump's Presidential Advisory Commission on Election Integrity in 2017-2018. The commission was disbanded after seven months without producing a report or finding evidence of widespread voter fraud. Internal documents revealed that the section of the draft report meant to present evidence of fraud was "glaringly empty."

He ran the Interstate Crosscheck voter purge system as secretary of state, which flagged 7.2 million voters across 30 states and led to an estimated 1.1 million voter registrations being purged before the 2016 election. The system had a false positive rate exceeding 99.99% and disproportionately flagged minority voters. It was suspended indefinitely in 2019 as part of an ACLU lawsuit settlement.

As attorney general, Kobach built the state's first fentanyl enforcement team with K-9 units, secured $6.9 million in state funding, distributed $8.5 million in addiction treatment grants to 32 organizations, and converted unused news racks into naloxone distribution boxes. He also won significant federal court victories, including a nationwide injunction blocking Biden's Title IX transgender regulations and a ruling striking down a Biden immigration program.

The Federal Election Commission fined Kobach $30,000 in 2022 for campaign finance violations during his 2020 Senate race. His campaign rented the email list of We Build the Wall -- the Steve Bannon-linked border wall organization where Kobach served as general counsel and board member -- for $2,000, far below market rate. Bannon was later indicted for fraud related to the organization (pardoned by Trump), and organizer Brian Kolfage was sentenced to 51 months in prison.

Before becoming a statewide officeholder, Kobach earned more than $800,000 as an immigration attorney drafting anti-immigration ordinances for cities across the country. ProPublica documented a 2-6 win-loss record in those federal cases. The cities that hired him were left with millions in legal bills -- Farmers Branch, Texas owed $7 million, and Hazleton, Pennsylvania had to seek a state bailout. None of those ordinances remain on the books.

Kobach won the 2022 attorney general race by just 1.7 points -- the closest statewide race in Kansas that cycle -- on the same ballot where Kansas voters rejected an anti-abortion constitutional amendment 59-41 and reelected Democratic Governor Laura Kelly. He significantly underperformed the typical Republican statewide margin.
whereTheyWorship:
Kobach attends Morning Star Church in Lawrence, Kansas, an evangelical congregation affiliated with Morning Star International, a worldwide movement based in Nashville. The church was founded in 1992 by Rev. John McDermott with eight members meeting in a living room. Kobach publicly denounced vandalism at "his church," confirming his personal connection to the congregation. The church's pastor has lobbied the Kansas Legislature on various issues.

Kobach has been notably open about bringing faith into government. As secretary of state, he hosted prayer meetings in his government offices and religious pamphlets were distributed weekly in his office. A religious discrimination lawsuit was filed related to office practices, though his office was ultimately cleared. He was married at St. Michael's Episcopal Church in Kansas City in 2001, and one earlier report linked him to Open Door Baptist Church in Kansas City as of 2018.

church.name: Morning Star Church
church.denomination: Evangelical / Non-denominational (affiliated with Morning Star International)
church.details: Located at 998 N 1771 Rd, Lawrence, KS 66049. Founded in 1992 by Rev. John McDermott.

quotes.text: Today marks an historic turning point in Kansas. Voters will now get to decide whether to reclaim the right to vote for justices, which they enjoyed from statehood until 1958.
quotes.source: Kris Kobach campaign statement on judicial selection amendment

quotes.text: People, not only lawyers in smoke-filled back rooms, should have a voice in selecting Kansas Supreme Court justices.

Our government routinely judges situations where you might regard people completely affecting themselves... like for example the use of controlled substances, like for example polygamy, that is voluntarily entered into. We condemn those activities even though they are not hurting other people, at least directly.
quotes.source: LGBTQ Nation (at the 2012 Republican National Convention, arguing against ending support for the Defense of Marriage Act)

quotes.text: If you really want to create a job tomorrow, you can remove an illegal alien today.

campaignFinance.totalRaised: $337,330 (2025 cycle); $502,626 cash on hand as of January 1, 2026
campaignFinance.narrative:
Kobach entered the 2026 race with the highest January 1 cash-on-hand total ever reported by a Kansas attorney general candidate. His 2025 fundraising of $337,330 came with modest spending of just $52,559, leaving him sitting on more than half a million dollars. The accessible donor records show a base of small-dollar Kansas contributors giving $10 to $100, which suggests broad grassroots support rather than dependence on a few large donors. However, the full Schedule A donor list is much longer than the small sample in the publicly accessible state filing.

Kobach's financial history includes a $30,000 FEC fine from his 2020 Senate campaign for receiving a below-market-rate email list rental from We Build the Wall, which the FEC classified as an illegal in-kind corporate contribution. His earlier financial disclosures as secretary of state were found by ProPublica to be incomplete -- his forms failed to list all clients paying him for immigration legal work, listing only one municipality while receiving payments from several others. He earned more than $800,000 total from immigration-related legal consulting between 2005 and 2018, including at least $150,000 while serving as secretary of state.
campaignFinance.undisclosed: The publicly accessible portion of the Kansas ethics filing shows only small-dollar donors. The full itemized donor list for the 2026 cycle is available through the Kansas Public Disclosure Commission but was not fully enumerable from the scanned PDF report. Kobach's earlier campaigns drew significant funding from immigration-enforcement organizations including FAIR and IRLI. His 2020 Senate campaign was fined $30,000 by the FEC for an undisclosed in-kind contribution from We Build the Wall.
